eventMetadata.context_description: World No. 1 Jannik Sinner leads trader consensus at 49.5% implied probability for the 2026 Wimbledon men's title, fueled by his unmatched 2026 dominance including five straight ATP Masters 1000 triumphs—most recently crushing Alexander Zverev 6-1, 6-2 in the Madrid final six days ago to surpass 14,000 ranking points—and ongoing bid for a record sixth in Rome. Carlos Alcaraz sits second at 18.5% amid a solid 22-3 season but hampered by recent withdrawals from Rome and Roland Garros as defending champion. Novak Djokovic lags at 8.0% following a troubling Italian Open second-round upset loss to qualifier Dino Prizmic two days ago amid lingering shoulder injury woes and physical struggles at age 39, despite his seven Wimbledon crowns. Grass-court history favors Sinner's 89% win rate, with home hopeful Jack Draper and Zverev rounding out next tier amid uncertain draws and surface transitions.

markets.question: Will Lovable be acquired before 2027?
markets.description:
This market will resolve to “Yes” if credible reporting confirms that any entity enters into an agreement to acquire the listed company by December 31, 2026, 11:59 PM ET. Otherwise, this market will resolve to “No”.

Mergers where the listed company is subsumed by another entity will count toward a "Yes" resolution.

An announced agreement between the listed company and an acquiring entity will qualify for a “Yes” resolution, regardless of whether the acquisition is ultimately completed.

The primary resolution source for this market is official information from the listed company and/or its leadership; however, a consensus of credible reporting will also be used.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus on Polymarket heavily favors Cursor at a 76% implied probability of acquisition before 2027, driven by SpaceX's April 21 announcement of a $60 billion option to buy the AI coding startup or fallback to a $10 billion partnership—after Microsoft scouted but passed—reflecting intense competition for developer tools amid AI infrastructure expansions akin to Google's $32 billion Wiz deal. Viking Therapeutics trades at 56% on big pharma buyout speculation for its GLP-1 obesity drug pipeline, fueled by CNBC reports and takeover target lists. Perplexity AI (25%) benefits from Google antitrust pressures boosting independent AI search plays, while GitLab (22%) and Zoom (17%) anticipate consolidation via Q2 earnings. Regulatory reviews of the SpaceX-Cursor option loom as pivotal catalysts.

markets.question: Will the Republican Party win the WI-05 House seat?
markets.description:
This market will resolve according to the party of the candidate who wins the WI-05 congressional district seat in the U.S. House of Representatives in the 2026 midterm elections. The midterm elections will take place on November 4, 2026.

​A candidate's party will be determined by their ballot-listed or otherwise identifiable affiliation with that party at the time all of the 2026 House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources. A candidate without a ballot-listed affiliation to either the Democrat or Republican parties will be considered a member of one of these parties based on the party with which they most recently expressed their intent to caucus at the time all of the House elections are conclusively called by this market's resolution sources.

This market will resolve based on the result of the election as indicated by a consensus of credible reporting. If there is ambiguity, this market will resolve based solely on the official results as reported by the United States government, specifically the Federal Election Commission (https://www.fec.gov/).

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus heavily favors the Republican Party at 84% implied probability to win Wisconsin's 5th Congressional District House seat, reflecting Rep. Scott Fitzgerald's incumbency advantage in a district rated Solid Republican by the Cook Political Report with an R+11 partisan lean. Fitzgerald won his last general election by 64.5% and faces no serious primary challengers ahead of the June 1 filing deadline and August 11 primaries. Democrats field Andrew Beck and Ben Steinhoff in their primary, but both lack the name recognition or fundraising to threaten a flip in this suburban Milwaukee stronghold, despite slight leftward trends in recent cycles. No recent polling or developments have shifted the dynamics, underscoring structural barriers to a Democratic upset absent a major national wave or scandal.
